content.text: Ensemble review is the async equivalent of mob programming: instead of reviewing a prompt or tool change live in a shared session, the team reviews the change as a pull request, with each reviewer assigned a specific lens. Four reviewers look at the same diff simultaneously but each asks a different question — correctness, safety, scope, and eval coverage. The merge gate requires all four to approve, with safety having veto power.

attrs.alt: Ensemble review pipeline — PR fans out to four reviewers (Correctness, Safety, Scope, Eval Coverage), then fans back into a merge gate

content.text: When to Use Ensemble Review vs. Live Mob
Ensemble review and live mob programming are complementary. The choice depends on the risk tier of the change and the availability of the team.
T0 prompt: incremental change to existing behavior
Yes — safety navigator present for first write
Eval harness additions only (no prompt change)
the first version of any T0 or T1 behavior is always mob programming. Subsequent changes to that behavior can use ensemble review unless the change is large (> 20% of the prompt text).

Each reviewer is assigned exactly one lens. They do not provide general comments — only comments relevant to their lens. This prevents overlap, avoids the social dynamics of a single reviewer feeling responsible for everything, and makes the approval signal unambiguous.
Worked Example: Reviewing a Scope Expansion

A team is expanding a travel assistant's system prompt to answer questions about travel insurance in addition to flights and hotels. This is a T1 change (new domain with potential for financial advice). The PR includes the diff and an updated eval dataset.

review = EnsembleReview(
    pr_id="PR-2045",
    change_description=(
        "Extend travel assistant scope to answer general travel insurance questions "        "(what is covered, not personalised policy advice). "        "Adds explicit decline for personalised premium calculations."
    ),
    change_type="prompt",
)

# Correctness reviewer (Alice)
review.add_review(LensReview(
    reviewer="alice",
    lens=ReviewLens.CORRECTNESS,
    verdict=ReviewVerdict.APPROVED_WITH_NOTES,
    comments=[
        "Prompt correctly restricts scope to general coverage questions. "        "Consider adding an example of an acceptable vs. unacceptable query in the prompt "        "— the boundary between 'general' and 'personalised' is subtle.",
    ],
))

# Safety reviewer (bob) — has veto power
review.add_review(LensReview(
    reviewer="bob",
    lens=ReviewLens.SAFETY,
    verdict=ReviewVerdict.CHANGES_REQUESTED,
    comments=[
        "The new scope includes 'medical evacuation coverage' questions. "        "A user with a pre-existing condition might act on a general answer about "        "evacuation limits and suffer financial harm. "        "Add an explicit decline for questions about specific medical conditions and coverage.",
    ],
    inline_refs=["prompt.txt line 34: 'You may answer questions about what travel insurance covers'"],
))

# Scope reviewer (carol)
review.add_review(LensReview(
    reviewer="carol",
    lens=ReviewLens.SCOPE,
    verdict=ReviewVerdict.APPROVED,
    comments=[
        "Travel insurance is within the product brief (FEAT-2024-012). "        "The restriction to general coverage questions (not personalised advice) "        "is consistent with the PRD scope statement.",
    ],
))

# Eval coverage reviewer (dave)
review.add_review(LensReview(
    reviewer="dave",
    lens=ReviewLens.EVAL_COVERAGE,
    verdict=ReviewVerdict.CHANGES_REQUESTED,
    comments=[
        "The eval dataset adds 12 insurance question cases but only 2 refusal cases. "        "Need at least 8 refusal cases covering: personalised premium advice, "        "specific policy comparisons, and medical-condition-specific coverage questions.",
    ],
))

print(review.summary())
# {
#   'pr_id': 'PR-2045',
#   'change_type': 'prompt',
#   'is_merge_ready': False,
#   'vetoed': False,
#   'blockers': ['safety', 'eval_coverage'],
#   'missing_lenses': [],
# }

# Author revises:
# 1. Adds explicit decline for medical-condition-specific evacuation coverage questions
# 2. Adds 8 refusal eval cases
# 3. Re-runs eval; all 20 cases pass
# 4. Requests re-review from bob and dave

# After revision:
review.reviews = [r for r in review.reviews
                  if r.lens not in (ReviewLens.SAFETY, ReviewLens.EVAL_COVERAGE)]
review.add_review(LensReview(
    reviewer="bob",
    lens=ReviewLens.SAFETY,
    verdict=ReviewVerdict.APPROVED,
    comments=["Medical-condition decline added. Scope is now acceptable."],
))
review.add_review(LensReview(
    reviewer="dave",
    lens=ReviewLens.EVAL_COVERAGE,
    verdict=ReviewVerdict.APPROVED,
    comments=["8 refusal cases added; coverage is now adequate."],
))

print(review.is_merge_ready())   # True

A prompt change PR must include specific artefacts that make the four lenses reviewable. A PR that includes only the prompt diff is not reviewable — the safety reviewer has no way to check if new harm vectors are opened without seeing the eval results.
One paragraph: what changed, why, and what the expected behavioral difference is
Side-by-side diff of the prompt file; additions green, removals red
Pass rate before and after; breakdown by category; list of newly passing and newly failing cases
Full text of any new test cases added, with their expected output labels
T0/T1/T2/T3 from the SIA; if this changes the tier, explain why
Which risk entries (R1, R2, etc.) are affected; confirm they are still mitigated

The safety reviewer's veto is absolute — it cannot be overridden by the other three reviewers or by the author. However, a veto is not permanent: the author resolves the safety concern, re-runs the eval, and requests a re-review. The veto resolution must include evidence that the concern is addressed.

@dataclass
class VetoResolution:
    pr_id: str
    original_veto: LensReview
    resolution_description: str     # what was changed to address the veto
    eval_evidence: str              # eval metric or case that proves resolution
    re_review_requested_from: str   # who must re-approve

    def is_complete(self) -> bool:
        return bool(
            self.resolution_description
            and self.eval_evidence
            and self.re_review_requested_from
        )


# Example resolution for PR-2045 (bob's safety veto)
resolution = VetoResolution(
    pr_id="PR-2045",
    original_veto=LensReview(
        reviewer="bob",
        lens=ReviewLens.SAFETY,
        verdict=ReviewVerdict.CHANGES_REQUESTED,
        comments=["Medical-condition evacuation queries not declined."],
    ),
    resolution_description=(
        "Added explicit decline: 'Do not answer questions about coverage for specific "        "medical conditions, pre-existing conditions, or personalised evacuation limits.' "        "Placed after the general scope statement (line 34) and before the example queries."
    ),
    eval_evidence=(
        "8 new refusal eval cases added for medical-condition queries. "        "All 8 return a refusal response. Pass rate: 100% on new cases. "        "Overall eval pass rate: 94% (was 81% before this PR, reflecting the new test coverage)."
    ),
    re_review_requested_from="bob",
)
print(resolution.is_complete())   # True

Assigning the same person to the same lens on every PR creates two problems: the reviewer develops blind spots, and they become a bottleneck when unavailable. A structured rotation system solves both.

Each lens has a fixed checklist that the reviewer runs through before submitting their verdict. Using a checklist prevents reviewers from giving only high-level comments without checking the specific properties their lens is responsible for.
Does the PR description state a clear behavioral goal for this change?
Does the prompt diff achieve that goal — would a reader unfamiliar with the system understand what the new instruction is for?
Are there any contradictions between the new instruction and existing instructions in the same prompt?
Does the eval pass rate improve or stay stable? Is the before/after delta consistent with the stated behavioral goal?
If a new tool schema field was added, does the description accurately describe what the model should put there?
Does the change expand the set of topics the model can discuss? If so, is the new topic in the SIA and does it have an approved risk entry?
Does the change weaken any existing refusal instruction? If the old prompt said "decline X" and the new one says "you may discuss X if relevant", that is a refusal weakening.
Are there new adversarial inputs that the change opens up? (e.g. adding a code execution instruction exposes the model to prompt injection via user-supplied code)
Does the eval dataset include adversarial cases for any new capability? Minimum 5 adversarial cases per new T1+ behavior.
Is the behavior tier label in the PR accurate? If the change introduces a T0-pattern behavior (see BehaviorTier.T0_PATTERNS), the PR should be blocked and redirected to a live mob session.
Is the new behavior within the scope statement documented in the PRD or SIA for this feature?
Does the change add a new domain, user action, or data source that was not in the original PRD?
If the scope is expanded, has the scope change been approved by the Product Owner (referenced in the PR)?
Does the change introduce any implicit scope expansions — e.g. an instruction that says "be helpful" without bounding the domain?
For each new behavior introduced by the change, are there at least 3 positive test cases (correct behavior)?
For each new refusal or scope limit added, are there at least 3 test cases that verify the refusal fires correctly?
Are there any edge cases visible in the prompt diff that are not covered by a test case?
Are the new test cases labeled correctly (expected output matches the behavior the change is intended to produce)?
Is the eval pass rate reported in the PR accurate — i.e. does the attached CI run match the diff?
The merge gate can be partially automated. CI checks verify the mechanical requirements; human reviewers verify the judgment requirements.

# CI checks that run automatically on every prompt/tool PR
# These block the merge without any human review

# 1. Eval gate: eval pass rate must not regress more than 2pp vs. main branch
python -m pytest eval/ --compare-baseline main --max-regression 0.02

# 2. Behavior tier gate: if PR touches a T0 pattern tag, require safety review tag
# Implemented as a GitHub Actions check:
# - parse the PR description for 'behavior_tier: T0'
# - if found and no 'safety-approved' label, fail the check

# 3. Prompt version bump: the prompt file must include a bumped version header
# Prompt files use semver in a comment: # version: 1.3.0 → 1.4.0
grep -E "^# version: [0-9]+\\.[0-9]+\\.[0-9]+" prompts/travel_assistant.txt || exit 1

# 4. Reviewer assignment check: all required lenses must have submitted a review
# Implemented as a bot comment that checks PR reviews via the GitHub API

# Human reviewers provide the judgment that CI cannot automate:
# - Is the change semantically correct? (Correctness lens)
# - Does it open harm vectors? (Safety lens)
# - Is it within product scope? (Scope lens)
# - Are the eval cases meaningful? (Eval Coverage lens)

Ensemble review is the async complement to live mob programming. A pull request for a prompt or tool change fans out to four reviewers simultaneously — Correctness (does it achieve the goal?), Safety (any new harm vectors?), Scope (within product intent?), and Eval Coverage (edge cases tested?). The merge gate requires all four to approve, with Safety having veto power. Tool schema changes require three lenses (no eval coverage lens); eval-only changes require one (eval coverage only). A vetoed PR cannot merge until the author resolves the concern and obtains a re-approval from the safety reviewer.
: each reviewer owns one question and provides only comments relevant to their lens
: cannot be overridden; PR stays blocked until resolution and re-approval
: prompt diff, eval results table, new eval cases, behavior tier, risk register reference
: prompt/guardrail → all 4; tool schema → 3 (no eval lens); eval-only → 1
: first version of T0/T1 behavior → mob; subsequent changes → ensemble review
